title: 1 Tahsas — St. Adam & The Assembly
narrative:
On this first day of Tahsas, the Church calls to remembrance the Father of the human race, the blessed Adam, whom God fashioned from the dust of the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, making him a living soul set over all creation. Adam, whose very name in the Hebrew tongue signifies 'earth' or 'red clay,' bore within his mortal frame the image and likeness of the immortal God; and though he fell through the subtlety of the adversary and was driven from the garden of delight, the mercy of the Most High did not abandon him, but rather appointed for his restoration a Redeemer who would clothe Himself in the selfsame clay. The Church of Alexandria commemorates also on this day the holy Prophet Elias, whom the Latins name Elijah, the Tishbite of Gilead, whose name signifies 'my God is the Lord'; this thundering herald of divine jealousy called down fire from heaven upon the prophets of Baal, was fed by ravens in the wilderness, and was translated bodily into the heavens in a chariot of fire, there to await his return as the forerunner of the last days.

In the second rank of this day's assembly stands the martyr-bishop Peter of Alexandria, who governed his flock with apostolic severity during the terrible persecution of Diocletian and sealed his witness with his own blood in the year three hundred and eleven. Peter is celebrated in the Coptic tradition as 'the seal of the martyrs,' for his death marked the close of the era of great persecutions and the dawning of the age of Constantine; his name, inherited from the rock-apostle of Christ, signified in his own person a steadfast foundation that no imperial fury could dissolve. The joining of these three witnesses — the first man, the fiery prophet, and the steadfast bishop — upon a single day declares the unbroken thread of God's covenant with humanity, stretching from the garden of Eden through the desert of Carmel to the prisons of Alexandria.

Each of these holy ones, in his own manner, bore the weight of expectation: Adam longing in his exile for the promised Seed who would crush the serpent; Elijah crying in the wilderness that the God of Israel is alone Lord; Peter refusing by his martyrdom to admit any sovereign above Christ the King. Their lives form a chorus of anticipation, a cry ascending through the ages toward the event this month is appointed to celebrate: the entry of the eternal Word into time, into clay, into the very substance of Adam's fallen race.

The theological seal of this first day of Tahsas is therefore the mystery of patient hope. The month opens not yet with the canticles of the angels but with the groaning of creation, the sighing of prophets, and the blood of witnesses — all of which testify that the world cannot save itself, that the Redeemer must come from beyond the veil of the possible. As Adam prefigures in his creation the new Adam who is Christ, as Elijah prefigures in his fiery zeal the Forerunner, and as Peter seals with his blood the age of expectation, so this first day of Tahsas sets the entire month upon its proper foundation: the longing of all flesh for the Word made flesh.
